title: Arithmetic Feature Interaction Is Necessary for Deep Tabular Learning (AMFormer)
authors: Yi Cheng, Renjun Hu (equal contrib.), Haochao Ying (corresponding), Xing Shi, Jian Wu, Wei Lin (Zhejiang University + Alibaba Group; work done during Cheng's internship at Alibaba under Hu)
tokenization_method: AMFormer does NOT propose a new tokenizer: it reuses the FT-Transformer/AutoInt input embedding verbatim — a 1-in-d-out linear layer for each numerical feature and a d-dim embedding lookup table for each categorical feature, giving one token per feature (X in R^{N x d}). The contribution is the 'Arithmetic Block' that replaces classic self-attention inside each of L transformer layers. Each layer runs TWO parallel attention streams: (1) an ADDITIVE stream = standard scaled-dot-product attention O_A = softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d)) V, which combines features additively; (2) a MULTIPLICATIVE stream that first maps embeddings to log space X_log = log(ReLU(X)+epsilon), runs the same attention there, then applies an exponential — so a weighted SUM in log space becomes a weighted PRODUCT in the original space, yielding multiplicative interactions (idea borrowed from Neural Arithmetic Logic Units, Trask 2018). Both streams use HARD top-k attention (keep the k largest QK^T entries per row, mask the rest to -inf so they vanish after softmax), enforcing sparse interaction with k independent of N. The two stream outputs O_A, O_M in R^{N x d} are vertically concatenated to 2N x d and fused by an FC layer along the candidate dimension back down to N x d (O = FC(VConcat([O_A,O_M])^T)^T), so every layer can express a full arithmetic combination. Residual connections + FFN are kept (unlike AutoInt, which dropped them).
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed). Numerical: per-feature 1-in-d-out linear embedding after z-score normalization. Categorical: d-dim lookup table (one row per category). No native missing-value mechanism. No temporal / time-varying support — rows are unordered static feature sets.
token_granularity: per-feature (one token per input column), inherited from the FT-Transformer/AutoInt backbone. Sequence length = N features. No [CLS] token; the head pools the enriched per-feature tokens. NOTE: with prompt-token optimization, the QUERY axis is replaced by Np learned prompt tokens, so the attention output has Np 'interaction-candidate' tokens rather than N — i.e., the prompt count, not the feature count, sets the candidate-token granularity.
numerical_strategy: raw-scalar affine embedding (FT-Transformer style): z-score normalize each column, then a learned 1-in-d-out linear map per feature. NOT piecewise-linear, NOT periodic/Fourier, NOT binned, NOT target-aware. The 'arithmetic' richness lives entirely in the attention block, not in the numerical encoder. The multiplicative stream additionally log-transforms the EMBEDDINGS (not the raw input) via log(ReLU(X)+eps).
categorical_strategy: plain learned embedding lookup table, one d-dim vector per category, identical to FT-Transformer. No target/CatBoost encoding, no hashing, no learned discrete codes.
high_cardinality_handling: none specific — a standard embedding table, so parameters and table size grow linearly with cardinality and rare/unseen categories are unhandled. This is a real gap for the 70+-feature high-cardinality setting; AMFormer offers nothing here beyond the vanilla lookup table.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, channel-as-token, or static/temporal fusion. The MI (MSLR-WEB10K learn-to-rank) and EP datasets are treated as i.i.d. tabular rows.
scales_to_many_features: GENUINE and the strongest part of the paper for this topic. The naive arithmetic block is O(N^2 d) (worse than FT-Transformer because of the second stream) and on the EP dataset (2000 features) un-prompted AMFormer took >20-25 hr and 36-38 GB GPU. The prompt-token optimization REPLACES the data-dependent query Q (N x d) with Np learned prompt tokens (P in R^{Np x d}), making attention O(Np x N x d) = LINEAR in N when Np is fixed. Measured on EP: ~7x faster training (25.77->2.93 hr for AMF-A; 20.88->3.11 hr for AMF-F), ~90-94% fewer GFLOPs, and GPU memory 33-38 GB -> 17-18 GB (~52% of original). Recommended Np=N for a few-hundred features; for large datasets start Np=256 or 512 and HALVE per layer. So unlike most tabular transformers it has demonstrated, measured efficiency at 2000 features — directly relevant to the 70+-feature goal.
downstream_model: transformer encoder (L sequential Arithmetic-Block layers + residual + FFN), then a classification or regression head. Realized as a DROP-IN MODULE inserted into two backbones: AMF-A = AutoInt + arithmetic block (d=32, 8 heads), AMF-F = FT-Transformer + arithmetic block (d=192, 8 heads). Default L=3 (universal across all four datasets), top-k=8, Np=256. Adam, lr warms to 1e-3 over 1k steps then decays 90% every 20k steps.
pretraining_objective: none. Fully supervised end-to-end (cross-entropy / MSE). No self-supervision, no transfer, no cross-table pretraining.
key_results: Synthetic GAM-style data (8 features, response = additive mixture of sparse multi-order monomials r=sum_i alpha_i prod_j x_j^{beta_ij}): vs classic Transformer, AMFormer is +1% to +57% accuracy as #classes C grows 4->512 (fine-grained modeling), +26-42% with limited training data (efficiency), +31-46% on minority classes (generalization); XGBoost collapses by C=64 because it only uses raw feature values. Real-world (4 datasets): mean performance rank AMF-F = 1.3 +/- 0.5, AMF-A = 2.0 +/- 0.8, beating FT-Transformer (4.5), DCAP (4.3), NODE (5.0), XGBoost (5.3), AutoInt (6.0), DCN-V2 (7.8). EP 89.83 / HC 75.67 AUC / CO 97.26 / MI 0.5557 MSE for AMF-F. The module improves both backbones on every dataset (one exception: AMF-A slightly below DCAP on HC), and crucially both AMFormer variants beat XGBoost on ALL four datasets whereas the raw backbones do not.
baselines_compared: XGBoost; NODE (differentiable trees); DCN-V2; DCAP; AutoInt; FT-Transformer. AMFormer enters as a plug-in producing AMF-A (AutoInt backbone) and AMF-F (FT-Transformer backbone). Baselines use original-paper recommended hyperparameters; XGBoost follows the FT-Transformer paper's protocol.
transferable_idea: Add an explicit MULTIPLICATIVE attention stream (attention in log space + exp = learned weighted products) running parallel to ordinary additive attention, then fuse via an FC over a 2N candidate axis — giving a per-feature transformer the ability to synthesize ratio/product features (BMI, T3/T4-style) that pure additive attention cannot. Pair it with (a) hard top-k attention for sparse interaction (k independent of N) and (b) prompt-token queries that make cost linear in N and stabilize data-invariant interaction patterns. It is architecture-agnostic (bolts onto AutoInt or FT-Transformer) so it can ride on top of whatever feature tokenizer the 70+-feature pipeline already uses.
limitations: Author-stated: multiplicative attention alone can be unstable (FT-Trans multiplicative-only ablation collapsed to 50.05% = chance on EP); prompt count Np must be tuned (too small underfits, too large overfits/redundant); top-k and L need per-dataset choice though L=3, k=8 generalize well. Found on read: (a) NO tokenization novelty — the embedding is FT-Transformer's, so AMFormer is a feature-INTERACTION method, not a tokenization method; (b) NO high-cardinality, missing-value, or temporal handling; (c) only 4 real datasets, single reported run (no variance/significance test on the per-dataset numbers, only rank std); (d) log(ReLU(X)+eps) zeros out all negative embedding coordinates before the multiplicative stream, an information-losing, sign-blind nonlinearity.
pitfalls: 1) Do NOT cite AMFormer as a tokenization advance — its tokenizer is literally FT-Transformer's lookup+linear; the claim space is interaction modeling. 2) The multiplicative stream is fragile: on FT-Transformer/EP the multiplicative-only variant gives 50.05% (pure chance), so the product branch can dominate and destroy training unless balanced by the additive stream and the FC fusion — a real reproducibility risk at 70+ correlated features. 3) The headline +57% / +20% gains are on a SYNTHETIC dataset purpose-built from multiplicative monomials (r = product of x_j^beta) — i.e., the data-generating process is exactly what a multiplicative operator is designed to fit; this is suggestive, not proof that real 70+-feature tables need multiplicative interaction. 4) log(ReLU(X)+eps) is sign-blind and eps-sensitive; embeddings with many negative coordinates lose information and gradients near zero are large. 5) Real-world eval is 4 datasets, single split, no repeated-seed variance — rank gaps under ~0.5 (e.g., AMF-A vs FT-Trans on HC) are not clearly significant. 6) Prompt-token speedup is real but the 'scalability' is about ATTENTION cost; the per-feature embedding tables for high-cardinality categoricals still scale with cardinality independent of prompts.
relevance_to_topic: Primary route feature-interaction-selection: a cheap, backbone-agnostic module that (a) adds multiplicative/ratio interactions a pure additive transformer cannot express, and (b) via prompt-token queries gives MEASURED linear-in-N scaling at 2000 features — the rare tabular-transformer paper with hard efficiency evidence in the wide-feature regime this topic targets. Its weakness for the topic is that it contributes nothing to tokenization itself (no categorical/high-cardinality/temporal handling).
code_available: yes — official PyTorch repo https://github.com/aigc-apps/AMFormer (Alibaba AIGC org, GPL-3.0, ~41 stars, 10 forks, ~10 commits, no releases/checkpoints; includes training code + dataset/model configs with sum_num_per_group / prod_num_per_group / cluster-attention params matching the paper; datasets must be fetched externally; maintenance status unclear).
